title: Web Application Firewall (WAF)
description: Filters HTTP/HTTPS traffic to protect web applications from OWASP Top 10 attacks.
fullExplanation: A WAF sits in front of web applications and inspects HTTP/HTTPS requests and responses. It blocks attacks like SQL injection, XSS, CSRF, and path traversal by matching request patterns against rule sets. WAFs can operate in detection mode (log only) or prevention mode (block). Cloud WAFs (Cloudflare, AWS WAF) are deployed at the edge. On-premise WAFs (ModSecurity, F5 ASM) run on dedicated hardware or as reverse proxies.

protectedAttacks.description: Detect SQL keywords in parameters — block or sanitize
Detect script tags and event handlers in input
Validate CSRF tokens, check Referer/Origin headers
Limit requests per IP, detect bot patterns
Comprehensive coverage of most common web vulnerabilities

deploymentModes.description: WAF sits in front of web server — all traffic passes through it
Inline without IP change — transparent to clients and servers
DNS points to WAF provider (Cloudflare, Akamai) — traffic scrubbed at edge
Module on web server (ModSecurity for Apache/Nginx)

vendors.notes: Easy setup, global CDN, free tier available
Integrates with CloudFront, ALB, API Gateway
Apache/Nginx module, OWASP Core Rule Set

keyFacts: WAF is not a substitute for secure coding — it's a last line of defense
False positives: legitimate requests blocked — requires tuning
Virtual patching: WAF blocks known vulnerability before code is patched
OWASP ModSecurity Core Rule Set (CRS): free, comprehensive rule set
WAF bypass: attackers encode payloads to evade signature matching
API WAF: protect REST/GraphQL APIs — validate JSON/XML schemas
